does: This paper proposes Green Shielding, a framework for assessing how large language models respond to benign, non-adversarial variations in user inputs rather than adversarial attacks. The authors introduce the CUE criteria—benchmarks with authentic Context, reference standards capturing true Utility, and realistic Elicitation perturbations—to systematically study input sensitivity. They instantiate this approach in medical diagnosis through HealthCareMagic-Diagnosis (HCM-Dx), a benchmark constructed from real patient queries with structured reference diagnosis sets and clinically grounded evaluation metrics. The work demonstrates that routine prompt-level variations significantly shift model behavior, revealing vulnerabilities in deployment that red-teaming typically misses.
connects: This work responds to the limitation of existing adversarial red-teaming approaches, which focus on worst-case attacks but overlook how models degrade under natural input variation. It builds on evaluation frameworks that emphasize ecological validity and real-world use cases rather than synthetic adversarial scenarios. The paper extends prior work on benchmark design by introducing domain-specific, human-centered criteria for medical AI evaluation. It connects to growing recognition that AI safety must address distribution shift and robustness beyond adversarial perturbations, combining insights from both ML fairness and clinical decision-support research. The collaboration with practicing physicians grounds the work in established concerns about variability in how clinical information is presented.
enables: This framework enables development of more nuanced deployment guidelines that account for routine variation in how users interact with LLMs. It supports building domain-specific evaluation benchmarks in medicine and other high-stakes fields where authentic, utility-driven metrics matter. The work opens pathways for characterizing which input variations are most concerning and developing targeted mitigation strategies that preserve model utility while improving stability. It also establishes methodology for studying prompt robustness at scale, potentially informing better prompting strategies, model fine-tuning approaches, and system design. The Green Shielding agenda suggests a broader shift toward user-centric AI safety evaluation that complements adversarial robustness with real-world stability analysis across diverse deployment contexts.
